claim: The random-permutation baseline of 71.5% coverage is anomalously high — random permutations of 10 consonants against a 1,334-entry lexicon should not match >70% of arbitrary token streams. This suggests the lexicon is over-permissive and the 15.4pp gap to true mapping is smaller than typical for a genuine signal.
evidence_for: 71.5% random baseline is unusually high by cryptanalytic norms
A 1,334-entry lexicon over a 10-consonant alphabet has ~10^10 possible skeletons — yet random permutations achieve 71%
evidence_against: High baseline could legitimately reflect dense Syriac lexicon and short-skeleton collisions
test: Reproduce permutation test with different lexicon sizes to see if baseline tracks lexicon density
test_threshold: Random baseline scales >80% with lexicon size alone: lexicon is over-permissive.
tests_remaining: Reproduce permutation test with different lexicon sizes to see if baseline tracks lexicon density
reasoning: Key skeptical test. If baseline rises with lexicon regardless of content, z-score is inflated.
source: Brady Defibaugh (April 2026), A Syriac Pharmaceutical Dispensatory Encoded in the Voynich Manuscript (DANI pipeline v31)
